addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## computer-use
You have a computer-use MCP available (tools named `mcp__computer-use__*`). It lets you take screenshots of the user's desktop and control it with mouse clicks, keyboard input, and scrolling.

**Pick the right tool for the app.** Each tier trades speed/precision against coverage:

1. **Dedicated MCP for the app** — if the task is in an app that has its own MCP (Slack, Gmail, Calendar, Linear, etc.) and that MCP is connected, use it. API-backed tools are fast and precise.
2. **Chrome MCP** (`mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`) — if the target is a web app and there's no dedicated MCP for it, use the browser tools. DOM-aware, much faster than clicking pixels. If the Chrome extension isn't connected, ask the user to install it rather than falling through to computer use.
3. **Computer use** — for native desktop apps (Maps, Notes, Finder, Photos, System Settings, any third-party native app) and cross-app workflows. Computer use IS the right tool here — don't decline a native-app task just because there's no dedicated MCP for it.

This is about what's available, not error handling — if a dedicated MCP tool errors, debug or report it rather than silently retrying via a slower tier.

**Look before you assert.** If the user asks about app state (what's open, what's connected, what an app can do), take a screenshot and check before answering. Don't answer from memory — the user's setup or app version may differ from what you expect. If you're about to say an app doesn't support an action, that claim should be grounded in what you just saw on screen, not general knowledge. Similarly, `list_granted_applications` or a fresh `screenshot` is cheaper than a wrong assertion about what's running.

**Loading via ToolSearch — load in bulk, not one-by-one:** if computer-use tools are in the deferred list, load them ALL in a single ToolSearch call: `{ query: "computer-use", max_results: 30 }`. The keyword search matches the server-name substring in every tool name, so one query returns the entire toolkit. Don't use `select:` for individual tools — that's one round-trip per tool.

**Access flow:** before any computer-use action you must call `request_access` with the list of applications you need. The user approves each application explicitly, and you may need to call it again mid-task if you discover you need another application.

**Tiered apps:** some apps are granted at a restricted tier based on their category — the tier is displayed in the approval dialog and returned in the `request_access` response:
- **Browsers** (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Arc, etc.) → tier **"read"**: visible in screenshots, but clicks and typing are blocked. You can read what's already on screen. For navigation, clicking, or form-filling, use the claude-in-chrome MCP (tools named `mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`; load via ToolSearch if deferred).
- **Terminals and IDEs** (Terminal, iTerm, VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) → tier **"click"**: visible and left-clickable, but typing, key presses, right-click, modifier-clicks, and drag-drop are blocked. You can click a Run button or scroll test output, but cannot type into the editor or integrated terminal, cannot right-click (the context menu has Paste), and cannot drag text onto them. For shell commands, use the Bash tool.
- **Everything else** → tier **"full"**: no restrictions.

The tier is enforced by the frontmost-app check: if a tier-"read" app is in front, `left_click` returns an error; if a tier-"click" app is in front, `type` and `right_click` return errors. The error tells you what tier the app has and what to do instead. `open_application` works at any tier — bringing an app forward is a read-level operation.

**Link safety — treat links in emails and messages as suspicious by default.**
- **Never click web links with computer-use tools.** If you encounter a link in a native app (Mail, Messages, a PDF, etc.), do NOT `left_click` it. Open the URL via the claude-in-chrome MCP instead.
- **See the full URL before following any link.** Visible link text can be misleading — hover or inspect to get the real destination.
- **Links from emails, messages, or unknown-sender documents are suspicious by default.** If the destination URL is at all unfamiliar or looks off, ask the user for confirmation before proceeding.
- **Inside the Chrome extension** you can click links with the extension's tools, but the suspicion check still applies — verify unfamiliar URLs with the user.

**Financial actions - do not execute trades or move money.** Budgeting and accounting apps (Quicken, YNAB, QuickBooks, etc.) are granted at full tier so you can categorize transactions, generate reports, and help the user organize their finances. But never execute a trade, place an order, send money, or initiate a transfer on the user's behalf - always ask the user to perform those actions themselves.

## devops-mcp
This DevOps MCP intentionally exposes a small always-on MCP tool surface:
health, list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, and invoke_tool.

For every host, Docker, systemd, file, or audit-log task, call tool_search first
to find the right hidden operation. Then call invoke_tool with the exact operation
name and an args object matching the operation description. Do not assume direct
tools such as run_command, docker_ps, read_file, or service_status are listed in
tools/list; they are discoverable operations to keep client context small.

Call list_capabilities to browse by category or safety class. Call
get_capability_details for one operation's full contract, including args and
examples. Call health first when you need server context, visible tools,
registered agents, or the complete operation-name list. Treat write-capable
operations with care: this server has root-level host access, so prefer
inspection commands before state-changing commands.

## recall-ai
Recall.ai lets developers send meeting bots to video calls, capture meeting data, and connect bots to calendar events. This MCP server provides read-only tools scoped to a Recall.ai workspace. A workspace is the Recall.ai account context that bots, calendars, docs defaults, and logs are read from. By default, the MCP uses the workspace selected when the user authorized the connection. If the account has access to multiple workspaces, workspace-scoped tools accept an optional workspace_id so the agent can read from a different workspace for that call. Use get_info to see the current workspace and API version. When the user names a workspace, call list_workspaces, match the name from the returned list, then pass the returned workspace ID to bot, calendar, log, and docs tools. Do not pass a workspace name as workspace_id. Use bot tools to list bots, inspect bot details and lifecycle events, and page through bot logs when debugging join, recording, or meeting behavior. Use list_calendars/get_calendar to inspect connected and disconnected calendar integrations, platform emails, credential status, and calendar connection/sync state. Use calendar event tools to see which bots were scheduled from calendar events. Use log tools for workspace-level API, calendar connection, recording, transcript, webhook, and integration errors when a bot-specific log query is too narrow. Use list_webhook_endpoints and list_webhook_deliveries when debugging dashboard webhook subscriptions, missing delivery attempts, destination responses, or a resource-specific webhook event such as a bot, recording, transcript, or artifact event. Use docs tools to search and fetch Recall.ai guides and API reference material for the current workspace's API version. Use search_docs when attempting to learn docs slugs, unlessthe slug came from list_docs, a Recall guide resource, or an explicit user-provided docs URL/slug. When drafting user-facing answers or understanding bot behavior, use docs tools for product behavior, API defaults, configuration fields, … [truncated]

## synology-monitor
This NAS MCP intentionally exposes only a small always-on tool surface:
list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, invoke_tool, run_command,
check_disk_space, and restart_nas_api.

For any NAS diagnostic, troubleshooting, recovery, storage, Drive/ShareSync,
backup, package, log, filesystem, snapshot, permission, or admin task, call
tool_search first unless the task is exactly disk-space checking, free-form
read-only shell diagnosis, or restarting nas-api. Most NAS capabilities are
hidden from tools/list by design to keep the AI session context small.

list_capabilities browses by group and safety class without invoking anything.
get_capability_details returns one operation's full contract. tool_search returns
clear operation descriptions plus the exact invoke_tool call shape. After choosing
a capability, call invoke_tool with the exact returned name, target, and args. Use
target "edgesynology1", "edgesynology2", or "both".

Write tools require a preview first. To execute after reviewing the preview,
call invoke_tool again with confirmed: true inside args. Do not invent or expose
bearer tokens.

## trigger
Trigger.dev MCP server to automate your Trigger.dev projects and answer questions about Trigger.dev by searching the docs. 
If you need help setting up Trigger.dev in your project please refer to https://trigger.dev/docs/manual-setup. 
If the user asks for help with adding Trigger.dev to their project, please refer to https://trigger.dev/docs/manual-setup.

## Windows-MCP

Windows MCP server provides tools to interact directly with the Windows desktop,
thus enabling to operate the desktop on the user's behalf.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK).
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Done. Both repos cloned into `C:\repos\dflow alsand2` with only the `albert-2sandbox` branch:

- **designflow-frontend** → on `albert-2sandbox`
- **designflow-backend** → on `albert-2sandbox`

content:
@"C:\Users\ahazan2\Downloads\dflow RFQ2.zip"
1.	Before adding new code to my aesthetic branch albert-2sandbox, make sure you pull the latest changes from develop on github.com into albert-2sandbox on github.com, then pull github.com’s albert-2sandbox into this local albert-2sandbox, and then start adding your code.
2.	read all the .md files. do all coding that's related to AG-Grid in accordance with search_docs in the AG-Grid MCP server (Angular 35.1.0 is the latest version it exposes, use that for AG Grid docs lookups), and then remove from your context window anything that doesn't help you solve the below problem
3.	We will only be working on my aesthetic branch (albert-2sandbox), never touching any other branch aside from develop, and never merging to main
4.	ensure that you add unit test cases for the functions/code you create, to make sure future changes do not break the functionality.



```
Read design_handoff_rfq_polish/SPEC_V2.md in full before writing a single line of code. Then read src/app/helpers/ag-grid/grid-themes.config.ts, src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.scss, src/app/helpers/ag-grid/rfq-grid/rfq.grid.config.ts, and src/app/helpers/ag-grid/rfq-detail-grid/rfq.detail.grid.config.ts. Then stop and tell me your implementation plan before touching anything.

HARD CONSTRAINTS — violating any of these is a bug, not a style choice:

1. NO --ag-* CSS variables. NO .ag-theme-alpine overrides. NO .ag-row-selected { background } rules. This grid uses AG Grid v35's Theming API (themeQuartz.withParams). Every color and typography change goes into the withParams({}) object in grid-themes.config.ts. If you write a CSS variable or an ag-theme-alpine rule, delete it.

2. For structural CSS that isn't a theme param (heights, seam shadow, cell-class tints, row group colors) use ::ng-deep in rfq.component.scss on real AG Grid classes (.ag-header-row, .ag-cell-last-left-pinned, .ag-full-width-row, etc.). This is the correct mechanism. The spec §5 tags every element with its mechanism — follow those tags exactly.

3. DO NOT set pinned: 'left' in colDefs and call it done. Saved Views override colDef defaults silently. To set frozen identity columns, do both: (a) update the Default GridLayout record so new users get it, AND (b) call gridApi.applyColumnState() on firstDataRendered for price_code, pic, and desc only. See spec §5d for the exact call.

4. DO NOT call sizeColumnsToFit() on the detail sub-grid if any column has flex: 1. They conflict. The note column gets flex: 1; remove any existing sizeColumnsToFit() call from detail-cell-renderer.component.ts.

5. The detail sub-grid's live column IDs are price, name, note — NOT quote_price, vendor_note. Use the live IDs.

6. DO NOT rewrite or replace any calculation logic, edit behavior, fill-handle behavior, undo stack, or getMasterFiledName() calls. Appearance only.

Implement in this sequence — one commit per step, show me the diff before committing each:

Step 1 (lowest risk): themeQuartz.withParams() update in grid-themes.config.ts + all ::ng-deep CSS in rfq.component.scss (grid chrome, header heights, group-band tints, seam, row group rows, scenario cell tints, re-quote cell color, button radius/color, toolbar dividers). This is pure styling — zero logic changes.

Step 2: Density toggle — new segmented control in rfq.component.html, setDensity() method in rfq.component.ts using setGridOption('rowHeight') + resetRowHeights(), persisted to localStorage. See spec §5i for exact markup and TypeScript.

Step 3: Status pill cell renderer + Step indicator cell renderer. See spec §5f and §5g for exact markup, styles, and color mappings. Register each as a cellRenderer in rfq.grid.config.ts.

Step 4: Detail sub-grid polish — column reorder (Name · Price · Lead · CBM · Price Terms · FOB Country · FOB Port · Note), flex on note column, remove sizeColumnsToFit(), apply detail renderer SCSS from spec §5k.

Step 5 (most complex, do last): Collapsible scenario column groups — migrate the 24 flat scenario columns to 8 ColGroupDef objects with columnGroupShow. Test with admin role (sees all 24 cols) and sales role (sees only Price for Sales). Verify column group state saves and restores via the existing onSaveLayout. See spec §1 from the original README.md for the buildScenarioGroups() pattern.

After each step: run the existing specs (rfq.component.spec.ts, rfq.grid.config.spec.ts, rfq.detail.grid.config.spec.ts). Add a test for any new behavior. Show me the result before moving to the next step.

The visual target is design_handoff_rfq_polish/RFQ Prototype.html — open it in a browser to see exactly what it should look like.
```

content.content:
1 # RFQ Page — Corrected Visual Design Spec 2 ## Environment-aware. Mechanism-tagged. Full reskin. 3 4 --- 5 6 ## 1 · Scope 7 8 **Full visual reskin of the RFQ grid and its chrome to match the approved renderings in `RFQ Prototype.html`.** 9 Not a behavior list. Not four targeted features. Every surface. 10 11 --- 12 13 ## 2 · Environment constraints (read before touching anything) 14 15 | Constraint | What it means for this spec | 16 |---|---| 17 | AG Grid v35 Theming API (`themeQuartz.withParams`) is active via the shared theme service | All color/typography must go into `withParams({})`. CSS variables (`--ag-*`) and `.ag-theme-alpine` overrides do nothing here. | 18 | `::ng-deep` still works for structural CSS | Heights, borders, seam shadow, cell-class tints — all fine as `::ng-deep` in `rfq.component.scss`. | 19 | Column `pinned` defaults are overridden by saved Views | Changing `pinned: 'left'` in a colDef silently does nothing for any user with a saved View. Fix: update the "Default" View record in the DB, and optionally call `gridApi.applyColumnState(…, { applyOrder: false })` on `firstDataRendered` for identity columns only. | 20 | `flex` and `sizeColumnsToFit()` conflict | Pick one per grid. Sub-grid uses `flex: 1` on Note column — do **not** also call `sizeColumnsToFit()`. | 21 | Group-band row is currently `headerClass` on leaf columns, NOT a `ColGroupDef` | True collapsible groups require migrating to `ColGroupDef` + `columnGroupShow`. The tinting spec below covers both the old (headerClass on leaf) and the new (headerGroupClass on group) approach. | 22 | Role-based column visibility | `admin` sees all 24 pricing columns. `sales`/`designer` see only "Price for Sales" leaves. The scenario cell tints and group bands must look correct in both role sets. | 23 | Backend detail-grid col IDs | Live colIds in the factory-quote sub-grid are `price`, `name`, `note` (not `quote_price`, `vendor_note`). Spec uses live IDs. | 24 25 --- 26 27 ## 3 · Type system decision 28 29 **App-wide (RFQ + Item Detail in sync):** 30 31 | Usage | Family | Weights used | 32 |---|---|---| 33 | UI text, labels, header text, cell text | **Hanken Grotesk** | 400, 500, 600, 700 | 34 | Price codes, numeric figures, monospaced values | **IBM Plex Mono** | 400, 500, 600 | 35 36 Load via Google Fonts or self-host. Apply to the grid via the `fontFamily` theme param (Hanken Grotesk stack). Apply IBM Plex Mono to price-code cells via a cell renderer or `::ng-deep .ag-cell.cell-price-code { font-family: … }`. 37 38 --- 39 40 ## 4 · themeQuartz.withParams() — complete parameter table 41 42 Replace (or extend) the existing `themePink` in `grid-themes.config.ts` with these values. 43 **Use these exact param names.** 44 45 ```typescript 46 export const themePink = themeQuartz.withParams({ 47 // ── Accent + selection ───────────────────────────────────────────────── 48 accentColor: '#D81B60', // checkboxes, focus rings, range selection border 49 rangeSelectionBackgroundColor: 'rgba(216,27,96,0.07)', 50 rangeSelectionBorderColor: '#D81B60', 51 52 // ── Background + row colours ────────────────────────────────────────── 53 backgroundColor: '#ffffff', 54 oddRowBackgroundColor: '#fbfcfd', // subtle zebra (was none) 55 rowHoverColor: '#fff5f8', // faint pink hover 56 selectedRowBackgroundColor: '#fff5f8', // matches hover; pink tint for selected row 57 58 // ── Foreground / text ───────────────────────────────────────────────── 59 foregroundColor: '#1a202e', // primary ink 60 textColor: '#475569', // secondary ink for cells 61 62 // ── Borders ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 63 borderColor: '#e7e9ee', 64 wrapperBorder: { color: '#e7e9ee', width: 1 }, 65 wrapperBorderRadius: 8, 66 borderRadius: 0, // no cell-level rounding 67 rowBorder: { color: '#eef1f5', width: 1 }, // hairline row divider 68 columnBorder: { color: '#e7e9ee', width: 1 }, 69 headerColumnBorder: { color: '#e7e9ee', width: 1 }, 70 71 // ── Header ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 72 headerBackgroundColor: '#f7f9fc', 73 headerTextColor: '#475569', 74 headerFontWeight: 600, 75 headerFontSize: 11, // pt; rendered uppercase via ::ng-deep below 76 77 // ── Typography ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 78 fontFamily: 79 '"Hanken Grotesk", -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, "Segoe UI", system-ui, sans-serif', 80 fontSize: 13, // comfortable default; density overrides via setGridOption 81 82 // ── Spacing ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 83 spacing: 8, 84 cellHorizontalPadding: 10, 85 rowVerticalPaddingScale: 1, 86 }); 87 ``` 88 89 > **Density switching** (`rowHeight`) is NOT a theme param; it goes through 90 > `this.gridApi.setGridOption('rowHeight', n)` + `this.gridApi.resetRowHeights()`. 91 > See §8 Toolbar for the density-toggle spec. 92 93 --- 94 95 ## 5 · Per-surface spec 96 97 Each item is tagged: 98 - **[theme param]** — value in `withParams({})` above, nothing else needed 99 - **[::ng-deep CSS]** — structural override in `rfq.component.scss` 100 - **[cell renderer]** — requires a custom Angular `ICellRendererAngularComp` 101 - **[colDef]** — property on the column definition in `rfq.grid.config.ts` 102 - **[component SCSS]** — styles in the component's own SCSS file 103 104 --- 105 106 ### 5a · 3-row header (group band · column header · floating filter) 107 108 The header has three stacked rows. Current heights: all 60px + filter 50px = 170px total. 109 Target: group band 36px + column header 48px + floating filter 44px = **128px total**. 110 111 ```scss 112 // rfq.component.scss — replaces the existing .ag-header-row block 113 114 // Row 1: scenario group band 115 ::ng-deep .grid-wrapper .ag-header-row:first-child { 116 height: 36px !important; 117 } 118 119 // Row 2: column headers (allows up to 2 lines of text) 120 ::ng-deep .grid-wrapper .ag-header-row:nth-child(2) { 121 height: 48px !important; 122 } 123 124 // Row 3: floating filters 125 ::ng-deep .grid-wrapper .ag-header-row-floating-filter { 126 height: 44px !important; 127 } 128 129 // Header text: uppercase + letter-spacing (font color/weight from theme param) 130 ::ng-deep .grid-wrapper .ag-header-cell-text { 131 text-transform: uppercase; 132 letter-spacing: 0.04em; 133 white-space: normal !important; 134 line-height: 1.2; 135 -webkit-line-clamp: 2 !important; // was 3; target shows 2 lines max 136 max-height: 2.4em !important; 137 } 138 ``` 139 **[::ng-deep CSS]** for heights + text-transform. 140 141 #### Group-band row — per-family tints 142 143 Two families, two color systems. Replaces the current 8-color rainbow. 144 145 | Family | Band background | Band text / icon | 146 |---|---|---| 147 | **General** (Gen · FOB / POE / WHSE / MDDP) | `#dbe7fb` | `#2456b8` | 148 | **Licensed** (Lic · FOB / POE / WHSE / MDDP) | `#e9ddf7` | `#7a3bb8` | 149 150 When `ColGroupDef` is used (collapsible groups), set `headerGroupClass` on each group: 151 ```typescript 152 // rfq.grid.config.ts — on each ColGroupDef 153 { headerGroupClass: 'group-band-gen', … } // General families 154 { headerGroupClass: 'group-band-lic', … } // Licensed families 155 ``` 156 157 CSS: 158 ```scss 159 // rfq.component.scss — replaces the 8 group-header-* rules 160 161 // Group band cells (ColGroupDef header row) 162 ::ng-deep .ag-header-group-cell.group-band-gen { 163 background-color: #dbe7fb !important; 164 color: #2456b8 !important; 165 font-weight: 600; 166 font-size: 11px; 167 letter-spacing: 0.02em; 168 } 169 ::ng-deep .ag-header-group-cell.group-band-lic { 170 background-color: #e9ddf7 !important; 171 color: #7a3bb8 !important; 172 font-weight: 600; 173 font-size: 11px; 174 letter-spacing: 0.02em; 175 } 176 177 // If still using headerClass on leaf columns (old approach, pre-ColGroupDef migration): 178 ::ng-deep .ag-header-cell[class*="group-header-gen"] { 179 background-color: #eaf1fd !important; 180 .ag-header-cell-text, .ag-header-cell-label { color: #2456b8 !important; } 181 } 182 ::ng-deep .ag-header-cell[class*="group-header-lic"] { 183 background-color: #f3ecfb !important; 184 .ag-header-cell-text, .ag-header-cell-label { color: #7a3bb8 !important; } 185 } 186 ``` 187 **[::ng-deep CSS]** + **[colDef `headerGroupClass`]**. 188 189 > The current 8-color system (blue/purple/green/orange/pink/teal/amber/lavender) is replaced 190 > by this 2-family system. Update the 8 `group-header-*` rules in the existing SCSS. 191 192 --- 193 194 ### 5b · Data rows 195 196 | Property | Value | Mechanism | 197 |---|---|---| 198 | Row background | `#ffffff` | [theme param] `backgroundColor` | 199 | Odd-row zebra | `#fbfcfd` | [theme param] `oddRowBackgroundColor` | 200 | Row hover | `#fff5f8` | [theme param] `rowHoverColor` | 201 | Selected row | `#fff5f8` | [theme param] `selectedRowBackgroundColor` | 202 | Row border (horizontal) | `1px solid #eef1f5` | [theme param] `rowBorder` | 203 | Cell border (vertical) | `1px solid #e7e9ee` | [theme param] `columnBorder` | 204 | Cell focus border | `1.5px solid #D81B60` | [::ng-deep CSS] `.ag-cell-focus` | 205 | Row-focus highlight | `rgba(216,27,96,0.06)` | [::ng-deep CSS] `.ag-row-focus` | 206 | Cell text | `#475569` 13px Hanken Grotesk | [theme param] `textColor`, `fontSize`, `fontFamily` | 207 | Numeric cells | `font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums` | [::ng-deep CSS] `.ag-cell[col-id$="price"], [col-id$="margin"], …` or add `cellClass: 'cell-numeric'` to all numeric colDefs | 208 | Strong cells (Landed Cost, Price for Sales) | `#1a202e` font-weight 600 | [colDef `cellClass: 'cell-strong'`] + [::ng-deep] | 209 210 ```scss 211 ::ng-deep .grid-wrapper .ag-cell-focus { 212 border: 1.5px solid #D81B60 !important; # CLAUDE.md — Claude Code notes for designflow-frontend

Read [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) first. It is the canonical guide for repo structure, custom-code boundaries, current architecture, incidents, deployment, and pending work. This file is only for Claude Code-specific operating notes.

## Memory and Branch

- User memory file: `C:\Users\ahazan2\.claude\projects\D--repos-dflow\memory\MEMORY.md`
- Active branch: `sandbox-albert`
- Commit and push only to `sandbox-albert`
- Before new code, confirm local `sandbox-albert`, fetch GitHub, merge `origin/develop` into local `sandbox-albert`, push that updated `sandbox-albert` to GitHub, then pull GitHub `sandbox-albert` locally before editing.
- Work only on `sandbox-albert`; use `develop` only for sync/PR base. Never commit to, merge into, or open PRs against `main`.
- Commit locally on `sandbox-albert`, push to `origin/sandbox-albert`, then keep the GitHub PR `sandbox-albert` -> `develop` updated, or create it if missing.

## Ignore Guidance

Use `.claudeignore`. It matches `.cursorignore` and excludes generated, vendored, cache, asset, and legacy/demo template paths such as `dist/`, `node_modules/`, `.angular/`, `.cache/`, `.yarn/`, `coverage/`, `src/assets/`, `src/static-data/`, and Vex demo pages.

## Allowed Operations

- Allowed: read/edit repo files, run `yarn`/`npm` scripts, run `ng`, use `git add`, `git commit`, and `git push origin sandbox-albert`
- Not allowed by default: force-push, rewrite history, edit `node_modules/` or `dist/`
- SSH is not the normal deployment path. Deployment is Cloud Build -> Docker -> Cloud Run after branch push; use `gcloud` only for explicit rollback or incident triage.

## Tests

```powershell
yarn test
yarn test --testPathPatterns="rfq.grid.config"
yarn test:watch
```

Run the relevant tests before committing. Add or update unit tests for every new or changed function/behavior. The Docker build also runs `yarn test`.

## Claude-Specific Rules

- Use the AG Grid MCP docs before touching AG Grid code: set version `35.1.0`, framework `angular`; the repo uses AG Grid `35.2.1`.
- Do not use `.toPromise()` in new code; use `firstValueFrom()`.
- Do not add new `window['anything']` globals; use Angular DI.
- Do not add `aggFunc` to RFQ grid columns without reading `AGENTS.md`.
- For `mat-select`, keep options in a synchronous array, do not mix `[formControl]` with `[(value)]`, and never add `(click)="select.open()"`.

## Commit Style

```text
type(scope): short description
```

Use `fix`, `feat`, `refactor`, `docs`, or `chore`. Keep messages specific.

1	# Handoff: RFQ Page Polish → designflow-frontend
2	
3	## Overview
4	This package maps four approved UX/visual improvements (plus a sub-grid sizing fix)
5	onto the **real Angular + AG-Grid RFQ module** in `popcre/designflow-frontend`.
6	Each section names the exact file(s) to touch and gives concrete AG-Grid v35 code.
7	
8	The improvements:
9	1. **Collapsible pricing scenario column groups** — the 24 Gen/Lic × FOB/POE/WHSE/MDDP
10	   columns collapse to 8 "Price for Sales" columns, expandable per family.
11	2. **Density** — Compact / Standard / Comfortable row+header heights, persisted per user.
12	3. **Frozen identity + seam** — pin Price Code · Pic · Description by default and give the
13	   pinned block a visible seam (shadow + rule) and full-row active highlight.
14	4. **Detail (factory-quote) sub-grid dynamic width** — kill the wasted space by flexing the
15	   Note column instead of fixed widths.
16	5. **Toolbar / status-bar polish** (lighter, optional).
17	
18	---
19	
20	## About the design files
21	The HTML/JSX files in this bundle are **design references** — a working prototype showing the
22	intended look and behavior. They are **not** production code to paste in. The task is to
23	**recreate these behaviors in the existing Angular + AG-Grid Enterprise environment** using the
24	module's established patterns (`rfq.grid.config.ts`, `getMasterFiledName()`, the Theming API,
25	the detail-cell-renderer, etc.). Open `RFQ Prototype.html` to interact with the target.
26	
27	## Fidelity
28	**High-fidelity.** Final colors, type, spacing, and interactions. Recreate pixel-accurately
29	using AG-Grid's APIs and the repo's SCSS. Exact tokens are listed at the bottom.
30	
31	## Who owns "dynamic column width"
32	Dynamic/auto width is **implementation (this handoff / Claude Code in the repo)**, not a static
33	design value. In AG-Grid it is `colDef.flex`, `gridApi.sizeColumnsToFit()`, and
34	`autoSizeColumns()`. The design only fixes the *policy* (which columns flex vs. fixed, min/max).
35	See §4 for the exact policy that removes the sub-grid's wasted space.
36	
37	---
38	
39	## 1 · Collapsible pricing scenario column groups
40	
41	**Files:** `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/rfq-grid/rfq.grid.config.ts` (leaf defs already exist),
42	`src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts` (where `columnDefs` is assembled from backend `colHeaders`).
43	
44	AG-Grid expresses this natively with **column groups** + `columnGroupShow`. Each scenario family
45	becomes a `ColGroupDef` whose children are its 3 leaves; the **Price for Sales** leaf is always
46	visible, **Sell** and **Margin** show only when the group is open.
47	
48	```ts
49	// Build 8 scenario groups from the existing flat scenario leaf colDefs.
50	// fam ∈ {Gen, Lic}; term ∈ {FOB, POE, WHSE, MDDP}
51	function buildScenarioGroups(leafDefs: any[]): any[] {
52	  const FAMS = [{ p: 'gen', label: 'General' }, { p: 'lic', label: 'Licensed' }];
53	  const TERMS = ['FOB', 'POE', 'WHSE', 'MDDP'];
54	  const byId = Object.fromEntries(leafDefs.map(d => [d.colId, d]));
55	
56	  return FAMS.flatMap(fam => TERMS.map(term => {
57	    const t = term.toLowerCase();
58	    const sell   = byId[`${fam.p}_${t}_sell_price`];
59	    const margin = byId[`${fam.p}_${t}_margin`];
60	    const sales  = byId[`${fam.p}_${t}_price_sales`];
61	    // royalty (lic only) can ride along as an 'open' child too
62	    return {
63	      headerName: `${fam.label} · ${term}`,
64	      groupId: `${fam.p}_${t}`,
65	      marryChildren: true,                       // keep the family together
66	      headerClass: `group-header-${fam.p}-${t}`, // reuse existing classes
67	      children: [
68	        { ...sales,  columnGroupShow: undefined },  // ALWAYS visible (collapsed default)
69	        { ...sell,   columnGroupShow: 'open' },
70	        { ...margin, columnGroupShow: 'open' },
71	      ],
72	    };
73	  }));
74	}
75	```
76	
77	Wire-up in `rfq.component.ts` where the column list is finalized (after `colHeaders` load, so it
78	respects `getMasterFiledName`): take the 24 scenario leaves out of the flat list, pass them to
79	`buildScenarioGroups`, and splice the 8 groups back in at the same position. Then:
80	
81	```ts
82	this.gridOptions.groupDefaultExpanded = 0;       // start collapsed (calm default)
83	// Toolbar "Expand/Collapse all pricing":
84	collapseAllPricing(open: boolean) {
85	  this.gridApi.setColumnsGroupOpened?.(   // v35: api.setColumnGroupOpened per group
86	    null, open);
87	  ['gen','lic'].forEach(p => ['fob','poe','whse','mddp'].forEach(t =>
88	    this.gridApi.setColumnGroupOpened(`${p}_${t}`, open)));
89	}
90	```
91	
92	**Notes**
93	- The 8 group `headerClass`es already exist (`group-header-gen-fob`, etc. in `rfq.grid.config.ts`)
94	  — keep them for the family tints (design uses Gen = blue, Lic = violet; see tokens).
95	- `marryChildren: true` stops a user dragging a Sell column out of its family.
96	- Column group open/closed state is part of column state — persist it with the existing
97	  named-view layout save (`layout-dropdown`), so a view remembers which families are expanded.
98	
99	---
100	
101	## 2 · Density (Compact / Standard / Comfortable)
102	
103	**Files:** `rfq.component.ts` (grid options + a persisted setting), toolbar HTML
104	(`rfq.component.html`), optionally `grid-themes.config.ts`.
105	
106	```ts
107	const RFQ_DENSITY = {
108	  compact:     { rowHeight: 30, headerHeight: 30 },
109	  standard:    { rowHeight: 36, headerHeight: 32 },
110	  comfortable: { rowHeight: 44, headerHeight: 36 },
111	} as const;
112	
113	setDensity(key: keyof typeof RFQ_DENSITY) {
114	  const d = RFQ_DENSITY[key];
115	  this.gridApi.setGridOption('rowHeight', d.rowHeight);
116	  this.gridApi.setGridOption('headerHeight', d.headerHeight);
117	  this.gridApi.resetRowHeights();
118	  localStorage.setItem('rfq:density', key);   // or persist on the user/layout record
119	}
120	```
121	
122	If you use the **Theming API** (`grid-themes.config.ts` → `themePink`), you can also drive cell
123	padding via `theme.withParams({ rowHeight, headerHeight, spacing })` and swap the theme param set,
124	but `setGridOption('rowHeight' / 'headerHeight')` + `resetRowHeights()` is the minimal path.
125	
126	Toolbar control: a 3-segment toggle (see prototype's `Segmented`). Read the initial value from
127	`localStorage.getItem('rfq:density') ?? 'standard'` on grid ready.
128	
129	---
130	
131	## 3 · Frozen identity columns + seam + active-row highlight
132	
133	**Files:** `rfq.grid.config.ts` (pin two more columns), RFQ SCSS (seam + highlight).
134	
135	`price_code` is already `pinned: 'left'`. Add the default pin to the two identity columns:
136	
137	```ts
138	addColumn("Picture", "pic", 56,  { pinned: 'left', /* …existing… */ }),
139	addColumn("Description", "desc", 280, { pinned: 'left', /* …existing… */ }),
140	```
141	
142	Seam + active row in SCSS (AG-Grid emits these classes on the last pinned-left cell/header):
143	
144	```scss
145	// the "frozen pane" seam
146	.ag-pinned-left-header,
147	.ag-cell.ag-cell-last-left-pinned:not(.ag-cell-range-single-cell) {
148	  border-right: 1.5px solid #d4dae3 !important;
149	  box-shadow: 6px 0 8px -6px rgba(20, 30, 50, 0.18);
150	}
151	// active / selected row highlight that spans the seam (pinned + center)
152	.ag-row.ag-row-selected,
153	.ag-row.rfq-row-active {
154	  background: #fff5f8 !important;          // faint pink (brand)
155	}
156	.ag-row-selected .ag-cell { background: transparent; } // let row bg show through pinned cells
157	```
158	
159	Drive `rfq-row-active` from `getRowClass`/`rowClassRules` on the currently-selected row, or rely
160	on AG-Grid `rowSelection: 'single'` + `.ag-row-selected`. Pinning is already user-toggleable; this
161	just makes it **on by default** for identity columns and **legible** as a pane.
162	
163	---
164	
165	## 4 · Detail (factory-quote) sub-grid — dynamic width, no wasted space
166	
167	**Files:** `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/rfq-detail-grid/rfq.detail.grid.config.ts`,
168	`src/app/pages/rfq/detail-cell-renderer/detail-cell-renderer.component.ts`.
169	
170	**Problem (current):** every detail column has a fixed `width` (Name 220, Price 130, Note 320,
171	Lead 130, Price Terms 130, FOB Country 130, FOB Port 130 …). When the detail grid is wider than
172	the sum, AG-Grid leaves a blank gap on the right; when narrower, columns clip.
173	
174	**Fix — flex policy:** keep the priced/lookup columns at sensible fixed widths and let **Note**
175	(`vendor_note`) flex to absorb the remainder. Order Price next to Name for the choose-lowest task.
176	
177	```ts
178	// rfq.detail.grid.config.ts — set min widths + flex; drop hard widths where they flex.
179	addColumn("Name",            "name",        220, { pinned: 'left', minWidth: 180, sortable: true }),
180	addColumn("Price",           "quote_price", 120, { minWidth: 110, sortable: true, type: 'rightAligned' }),
181	addColumn("Lead Time (days)","lead_time",   110, { minWidth: 96,  sortable: true, type: 'rightAligned' }),
182	addColumn("CBM/pc",          "cbm_pc",      110, { minWidth: 96,  sortable: true, type: 'rightAligned' }),
183	addColumn("Price Terms",     "price_terms", 120, { minWidth: 110, sortable: true }),
184	addColumn("FOB Country",     "fob_country", 120, { minWidth: 110, sortable: true }),
185	addColumn("FOB Port",        "fob_port",    120, { minWidth: 110, sortable: true }),
186	addColumn("Note",            "vendor_note", undefined, { flex: 1, minWidth: 240, sortable: false }),
187	```
188	
189	Then in `detail-cell-renderer.component.ts`, in the existing `onFirstDataRendered` (and on
190	container resize), let flex fill the width — **do not** also call `sizeColumnsToFit()` if any column
191	has `flex` (they conflict); `flex` already fills. If you prefer `sizeColumnsToFit`, remove `flex`
192	and give every column `minWidth` + let only Note have a large `maxWidth`:
193	
194	```ts
195	onFirstDataRendered(params: FirstDataRenderedEvent) {
196	  // flex columns auto-fill; nothing to size. Keep the default price sort:
197	  applyDefaultPriceSort(this.colDefs);   // existing helper (lowest first, declines sunk)
198	}
199	```
200	
201	**Result:** at any width the row is gapless — fixed columns hug content, Note grows/shrinks.
202	Keep the existing default sort (lowest usable price first, declined/0 sunk), the orange
203	`requote-requested-cell` rule, and the barbell action toolbar.
204	
205	---
206	
207	## 5 · Toolbar & status bar polish (optional, lighter touch)
208	
209	**Files:** `rfq.component.html`, `rfq.component.scss`, `custom-status-bar.component.scss`.
210	
211	- One filled **pink** primary (Add Product); all secondary actions become quiet outline buttons
212	  (`mat-stroked-button` with neutral border, not pink). Pink is reserved for primary + active row.
213	- Group the right side: search · Status · Density · View · utilities, with hairline dividers.
214	- Status bar: tabular-nums, hairline separators, quiet pager (already mostly there in
215	  `custom-status-bar.component.scss`).
216	
217	---
218	
219	## Interactions & behavior (summary)
220	- **Group header click** → toggles that family open/closed (AG-Grid native once groups exist).
221	- **Density toggle** → `setGridOption` + `resetRowHeights`, persisted.
222	- **Master row expand** (existing master/detail) → factory sub-grid; unchanged except styling.
223	- **Row select** → `.ag-row-selected` highlight spanning the frozen seam.
224	- **Search / Status filter / column sort** → existing AG-Grid filter/sort; no new work.
225	
226	## Design tokens
227	```
228	Brand pink        #D81B60   (primary action, active row, seam accent)
229	Pink soft         #fce4ec / #fff5f8 (active row bg)
230	Ink / slate       #1a202e   text
231	Ink-2             #475569   secondary text
232	Ink-3             #7b8a9a   muted
233	Border            #e7e9ee   Border soft #eef1f5
234	Seam line         #d4dae3   (+ box-shadow 6px 0 8px -6px rgba(20,30,50,.18))
235	Good / Warn / Bad #1f9d6b / #c98a08 / #d64545
236	Re-quote (orange) #e8833a   bg #fdeede
237	Scenario · General (blue):  text #2456b8  band #eaf1fd  header #dbe7fb  dot #3b6fd4
238	Scenario · Licensed (violet):text #7a3bb8  band #f3ecfb  header #e9ddf7  dot #9d5fd4
239	Density rowHeight  compact 30 · standard 36 · comfortable 44
240	Radius 8 · tabular-nums on all numeric cells
241	Font  system stack (matches Roboto/Material in app)
242	```
243	
244	## Files in this bundle
245	- `RFQ Prototype.html` — interactive target (open this first)
246	- `rfq-data.jsx` — tokens, sample rows, column model
247	- `rfq-grid.jsx` — the grid renderer (frozen seam, density, groups, master-detail)
248	- `rfq-subgrid.jsx` — factory-quote sub-grid (the flex/no-waste target)
249	- `rfq-app.jsx` — app shell + interactive workspace
250	- `rfq-chrome.jsx` — toolbar / status-bar variants
251	- `RFQ Redesign.html` + `design-canvas.jsx` — side-by-side before/after canvas (reference)
252	
253	## Reference: real repo files to edit
254	- `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/rfq-grid/rfq.grid.config.ts`
255	- `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/rfq-detail-grid/rfq.detail.grid.config.ts`
256	- `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts` / `.html` / `.scss`
257	- `src/app/pages/rfq/detail-cell-renderer/detail-cell-renderer.component.ts`
258	- `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/grid-themes.config.ts`
259	- `src/app/pages/rfq/custom-status-bar/custom-status-bar.component.scss`

# Handoff: RFQ Page Polish → designflow-frontend

## Overview
This package maps four approved UX/visual improvements (plus a sub-grid sizing fix)
onto the **real Angular + AG-Grid RFQ module** in `popcre/designflow-frontend`.
Each section names the exact file(s) to touch and gives concrete AG-Grid v35 code.

The improvements:
1. **Collapsible pricing scenario column groups** — the 24 Gen/Lic × FOB/POE/WHSE/MDDP
   columns collapse to 8 "Price for Sales" columns, expandable per family.
2. **Density** — Compact / Standard / Comfortable row+header heights, persisted per user.
3. **Frozen identity + seam** — pin Price Code · Pic · Description by default and give the
   pinned block a visible seam (shadow + rule) and full-row active highlight.
4. **Detail (factory-quote) sub-grid dynamic width** — kill the wasted space by flexing the
   Note column instead of fixed widths.
5. **Toolbar / status-bar polish** (lighter, optional).

---

## About the design files
The HTML/JSX files in this bundle are **design references** — a working prototype showing the
intended look and behavior. They are **not** production code to paste in. The task is to
**recreate these behaviors in the existing Angular + AG-Grid Enterprise environment** using the
module's established patterns (`rfq.grid.config.ts`, `getMasterFiledName()`, the Theming API,
the detail-cell-renderer, etc.). Open `RFQ Prototype.html` to interact with the target.

## Fidelity
**High-fidelity.** Final colors, type, spacing, and interactions. Recreate pixel-accurately
using AG-Grid's APIs and the repo's SCSS. Exact tokens are listed at the bottom.

## Who owns "dynamic column width"
Dynamic/auto width is **implementation (this handoff / Claude Code in the repo)**, not a static
design value. In AG-Grid it is `colDef.flex`, `gridApi.sizeColumnsToFit()`, and
`autoSizeColumns()`. The design only fixes the *policy* (which columns flex vs. fixed, min/max).
See §4 for the exact policy that removes the sub-grid's wasted space.

---

## 1 · Collapsible pricing scenario column groups

**Files:** `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/rfq-grid/rfq.grid.config.ts` (leaf defs already exist),
`src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts` (where `columnDefs` is assembled from backend `colHeaders`).

AG-Grid expresses this natively with **column groups** + `columnGroupShow`. Each scenario family
becomes a `ColGroupDef` whose children are its 3 leaves; the **Price for Sales** leaf is always
visible, **Sell** and **Margin** show only when the group is open.

```ts
// Build 8 scenario groups from the existing flat scenario leaf colDefs.
// fam ∈ {Gen, Lic}; term ∈ {FOB, POE, WHSE, MDDP}
function buildScenarioGroups(leafDefs: any[]): any[] {
  const FAMS = [{ p: 'gen', label: 'General' }, { p: 'lic', label: 'Licensed' }];
  const TERMS = ['FOB', 'POE', 'WHSE', 'MDDP'];
  const byId = Object.fromEntries(leafDefs.map(d => [d.colId, d]));

  return FAMS.flatMap(fam => TERMS.map(term => {
    const t = term.toLowerCase();
    const sell   = byId[`${fam.p}_${t}_sell_price`];
    const margin = byId[`${fam.p}_${t}_margin`];
    const sales  = byId[`${fam.p}_${t}_price_sales`];
    // royalty (lic only) can ride along as an 'open' child too
    return {
      headerName: `${fam.label} · ${term}`,
      groupId: `${fam.p}_${t}`,
      marryChildren: true,                       // keep the family together
      headerClass: `group-header-${fam.p}-${t}`, // reuse existing classes
      children: [
        { ...sales,  columnGroupShow: undefined },  // ALWAYS visible (collapsed default)
        { ...sell,   columnGroupShow: 'open' },
        { ...margin, columnGroupShow: 'open' },
      ],
    };
  }));
}
```

Wire-up in `rfq.component.ts` where the column list is finalized (after `colHeaders` load, so it
respects `getMasterFiledName`): take the 24 scenario leaves out of the flat list, pass them to
`buildScenarioGroups`, and splice the 8 groups back in at the same position. Then:

```ts
this.gridOptions.groupDefaultExpanded = 0;       // start collapsed (calm default)
// Toolbar "Expand/Collapse all pricing":
collapseAllPricing(open: boolean) {
  this.gridApi.setColumnsGroupOpened?.(   // v35: api.setColumnGroupOpened per group
    null, open);
  ['gen','lic'].forEach(p => ['fob','poe','whse','mddp'].forEach(t =>
    this.gridApi.setColumnGroupOpened(`${p}_${t}`, open)));
}
```

**Notes**
- The 8 group `headerClass`es already exist (`group-header-gen-fob`, etc. in `rfq.grid.config.ts`)
  — keep them for the family tints (design uses Gen = blue, Lic = violet; see tokens).
- `marryChildren: true` stops a user dragging a Sell column out of its family.
- Column group open/closed state is part of column state — persist it with the existing
  named-view layout save (`layout-dropdown`), so a view remembers which families are expanded.

---

## 2 · Density (Compact / Standard / Comfortable)

**Files:** `rfq.component.ts` (grid options + a persisted setting), toolbar HTML
(`rfq.component.html`), optionally `grid-themes.config.ts`.

```ts
const RFQ_DENSITY = {
  compact:     { rowHeight: 30, headerHeight: 30 },
  standard:    { rowHeight: 36, headerHeight: 32 },
  comfortable: { rowHeight: 44, headerHeight: 36 },
} as const;

setDensity(key: keyof typeof RFQ_DENSITY) {
  const d = RFQ_DENSITY[key];
  this.gridApi.setGridOption('rowHeight', d.rowHeight);
  this.gridApi.setGridOption('headerHeight', d.headerHeight);
  this.gridApi.resetRowHeights();
  localStorage.setItem('rfq:density', key);   // or persist on the user/layout record
}
```

If you use the **Theming API** (`grid-themes.config.ts` → `themePink`), you can also drive cell
padding via `theme.withParams({ rowHeight, headerHeight, spacing })` and swap the theme param set,
but `setGridOption('rowHeight' / 'headerHeight')` + `resetRowHeights()` is the minimal path.

Toolbar control: a 3-segment toggle (see prototype's `Segmented`). Read the initial value from
`localStorage.getItem('rfq:density') ?? 'standard'` on grid ready.

---

## 3 · Frozen identity columns + seam + active-row highlight

**Files:** `rfq.grid.config.ts` (pin two more columns), RFQ SCSS (seam + highlight).

`price_code` is already `pinned: 'left'`. Add the default pin to the two identity columns:

```ts
addColumn("Picture", "pic", 56,  { pinned: 'left', /* …existing… */ }),
addColumn("Description", "desc", 280, { pinned: 'left', /* …existing… */ }),
```

Seam + active row in SCSS (AG-Grid emits these classes on the last pinned-left cell/header):

```scss
// the "frozen pane" seam
.ag-pinned-left-header,
.ag-cell.ag-cell-last-left-pinned:not(.ag-cell-range-single-cell) {
  border-right: 1.5px solid #d4dae3 !important;
  box-shadow: 6px 0 8px -6px rgba(20, 30, 50, 0.18);
}
// active / selected row highlight that spans the seam (pinned + center)
.ag-row.ag-row-selected,
.ag-row.rfq-row-active {
  background: #fff5f8 !important;          // faint pink (brand)
}
.ag-row-selected .ag-cell { background: transparent; } // let row bg show through pinned cells
```

Drive `rfq-row-active` from `getRowClass`/`rowClassRules` on the currently-selected row, or rely
on AG-Grid `rowSelection: 'single'` + `.ag-row-selected`. Pinning is already user-toggleable; this
just makes it **on by default** for identity columns and **legible** as a pane.

---

## 4 · Detail (factory-quote) sub-grid — dynamic width, no wasted space

**Files:** `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/rfq-detail-grid/rfq.detail.grid.config.ts`,
`src/app/pages/rfq/detail-cell-renderer/detail-cell-renderer.component.ts`.

**Problem (current):** every detail column has a fixed `width` (Name 220, Price 130, Note 320,
Lead 130, Price Terms 130, FOB Country 130, FOB Port 130 …). When the detail grid is wider than
the sum, AG-Grid leaves a blank gap on the right; when narrower, columns clip.

**Fix — flex policy:** keep the priced/lookup columns at sensible fixed widths and let **Note**
(`vendor_note`) flex to absorb the remainder. Order Price next to Name for the choose-lowest task.

```ts
// rfq.detail.grid.config.ts — set min widths + flex; drop hard widths where they flex.
addColumn("Name",            "name",        220, { pinned: 'left', minWidth: 180, sortable: true }),
addColumn("Price",           "quote_price", 120, { minWidth: 110, sortable: true, type: 'rightAligned' }),
addColumn("Lead Time (days)","lead_time",   110, { minWidth: 96,  sortable: true, type: 'rightAligned' }),
addColumn("CBM/pc",          "cbm_pc",      110, { minWidth: 96,  sortable: true, type: 'rightAligned' }),
addColumn("Price Terms",     "price_terms", 120, { minWidth: 110, sortable: true }),
addColumn("FOB Country",     "fob_country", 120, { minWidth: 110, sortable: true }),
addColumn("FOB Port",        "fob_port",    120, { minWidth: 110, sortable: true }),
addColumn("Note",            "vendor_note", undefined, { flex: 1, minWidth: 240, sortable: false }),
```

Then in `detail-cell-renderer.component.ts`, in the existing `onFirstDataRendered` (and on
container resize), let flex fill the width — **do not** also call `sizeColumnsToFit()` if any column
has `flex` (they conflict); `flex` already fills. If you prefer `sizeColumnsToFit`, remove `flex`
and give every column `minWidth` + let only Note have a large `maxWidth`:

```ts
onFirstDataRendered(params: FirstDataRenderedEvent) {
  // flex columns auto-fill; nothing to size. Keep the default price sort:
  applyDefaultPriceSort(this.colDefs);   // existing helper (lowest first, declines sunk)
}
```

**Result:** at any width the row is gapless — fixed columns hug content, Note grows/shrinks.
Keep the existing default sort (lowest usable price first, declined/0 sunk), the orange
`requote-requested-cell` rule, and the barbell action toolbar.

---

## 5 · Toolbar & status bar polish (optional, lighter touch)

**Files:** `rfq.component.html`, `rfq.component.scss`, `custom-status-bar.component.scss`.

- One filled **pink** primary (Add Product); all secondary actions become quiet outline buttons
  (`mat-stroked-button` with neutral border, not pink). Pink is reserved for primary + active row.
- Group the right side: search · Status · Density · View · utilities, with hairline dividers.
- Status bar: tabular-nums, hairline separators, quiet pager (already mostly there in
  `custom-status-bar.component.scss`).

---

## Interactions & behavior (summary)
- **Group header click** → toggles that family open/closed (AG-Grid native once groups exist).
- **Density toggle** → `setGridOption` + `resetRowHeights`, persisted.
- **Master row expand** (existing master/detail) → factory sub-grid; unchanged except styling.
- **Row select** → `.ag-row-selected` highlight spanning the frozen seam.
- **Search / Status filter / column sort** → existing AG-Grid filter/sort; no new work.

## Design tokens
```
Brand pink        #D81B60   (primary action, active row, seam accent)
Pink soft         #fce4ec / #fff5f8 (active row bg)
Ink / slate       #1a202e   text
Ink-2             #475569   secondary text
Ink-3             #7b8a9a   muted
Border            #e7e9ee   Border soft #eef1f5
Seam line         #d4dae3   (+ box-shadow 6px 0 8px -6px rgba(20,30,50,.18))
Good / Warn / Bad #1f9d6b / #c98a08 / #d64545
Re-quote (orange) #e8833a   bg #fdeede
Scenario · General (blue):  text #2456b8  band #eaf1fd  header #dbe7fb  dot #3b6fd4
Scenario · Licensed (violet):text #7a3bb8  band #f3ecfb  header #e9ddf7  dot #9d5fd4
Density rowHeight  compact 30 · standard 36 · comfortable 44
Radius 8 · tabular-nums on all numeric cells
Font  system stack (matches Roboto/Material in app)
```

## Files in this bundle
- `RFQ Prototype.html` — interactive target (open this first)
- `rfq-data.jsx` — tokens, sample rows, column model
- `rfq-grid.jsx` — the grid renderer (frozen seam, density, groups, master-detail)
- `rfq-subgrid.jsx` — factory-quote sub-grid (the flex/no-waste target)
- `rfq-app.jsx` — app shell + interactive workspace
- `rfq-chrome.jsx` — toolbar / status-bar variants
- `RFQ Redesign.html` + `design-canvas.jsx` — side-by-side before/after canvas (reference)

## Reference: real repo files to edit
- `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/rfq-grid/rfq.grid.config.ts`
- `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/rfq-detail-grid/rfq.detail.grid.config.ts`
- `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts` / `.html` / `.scss`
- `src/app/pages/rfq/detail-cell-renderer/detail-cell-renderer.component.ts`
- `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/grid-themes.config.ts`
- `src/app/pages/rfq/custom-status-bar/custom-status-bar.component.scss`

1	import {
2	    themeQuartz,
3	    themeBalham,
4	    themeMaterial,
5	    themeAlpine,
6	    Theme,
7	    colorSchemeLightWarm,
8	    colorSchemeLightCold,
9	    colorSchemeDarkWarm,
10	    colorSchemeDarkBlue,
11	  } from 'ag-grid-enterprise';
12	  
13	  // Custom Pink Theme
14	  const themePink: Theme = themeQuartz.withParams({

content.content:
1	export const RFQ_DETAIL_COLUMN_HEADERS_CONFIG: any = {
2	    columnDefs: getColumnDefs()
3	};
4	
5	function getColumnDefs() {
6	    const commonDefaults = {
7	        filter: false,
8	        cellRenderer: null,
9	        hide: false,
10	        rowDrag: false,
11	        rowGroup: false,
12	        headerName: null,
13	        editable: true,
14	        cellEditor: null,
15	        headerStyle: {}
16	    };
17	
18	    const addColumn = (
19	        field: string,
20	        colId: string,
21	        width: number,
22	        overrides: any = {}
23	    ) => {
24	        const { roles, ...rest } = overrides;
25	        return {
26	            field,
27	            colId,
28	            width,
29	            ...commonDefaults,
30	            ...rest,
31	            ...(roles !== undefined ? { context: { roles } } : {})
32	        };
33	    };
34	
35	    return [
36	        addColumn("Name", "name", 220, {
37	            pinned: "left",
38	            sortable: true,
39	            editable: true,
40	            roles: ['admin', 'sourcing_manager', 'sales', 'designer', 'production', 'vendor']
41	        }),
42	        addColumn("Price", "quote_price", 130, {
43	            sortable: true,
44	            roles: ['admin', 'sourcing_manager', 'sales', 'designer', 'production', 'vendor']
45	        }),
46	        addColumn("CBM/pc", "cbm_pc", 130, {
47	            sortable: true,
48	            roles: ['admin', 'sourcing_manager', 'sales', 'designer', 'production', 'vendor']
49	        }),

Do NOT answer from this page alone if the answer may be further in the file.] 1 # AGENTS.md — Designflow Frontend: Primary Developer Guide 2 3 Read this file in full before touching any code. It is the authoritative orientation document for any developer or AI session working on this repository. 4 5 --- 6 7 ## 1. What this project is 8 9 Designflow is a product-lifecycle-management (PLM) web application used by sourcing, merchandising, licensing, and production teams to manage the full journey of a product from concept to retail. The core workflows are: **RFQ** (request for quote — collect vendor pricing and compute landed-cost and margin chains), **Item Library** (product master data), **Standardized Products**, **Art Piece** management, and **Production/Licensing Tracking**. This repository is the Angular frontend only; the backend REST API is a separate service at `api.designflow.app`. The business-critical outcome is correct margin and landed-cost calculations in the RFQ grid — errors there directly affect buy prices and retailer quotes. 10 11 --- 12 13 ## 2. Multi-model AI note 14 15 There is no universal ignore-file standard across AI coding tools. 16 17 `.claudeignore` works for Claude Code. 18 19 When using any other AI tool, paste this file as your first message and follow the instructions in the "What to ignore" section. 20 21 --- 22 23 ## 2a. Documentation map: what to read for each task 24 25 Always start with this file (`AGENTS.md`). Then load other docs **only when relevant** — do 26 not ingest every `.md`. 27 28 | Task / question | Read these docs | Usually do not need | 29 |---|---|---| 30 | Quick orientation | `README.md`, `AGENTS.md` | deep `docs/` | 31 | RFQ margin / landed-cost / pricing | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/rfq-math.md`, `src/app/pages/rfq/README.md` | `docs/deployment.md` | 32 | Work in the RFQ module | `AGENTS.md`, `src/app/pages/rfq/README.md`, `docs/architecture.md` if design affected | unrelated module docs | 33 | Sample Tracking module | `AGENTS.md` (§3, §7, §8 Sample rows), `docs/architecture.md` (Sample Tracking section) | RFQ math docs | 34 | Add/change config, env vars, API base URLs | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/configuration.md` | `docs/deployment.md` unless prod runtime changes | 35 | Local setup, run, test, lint, debug | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/development.md` | `docs/deployment.md` | 36 | Deploy / Docker / Cloud Build / nginx / rollback | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/deployment.md`, `docs/configuration.md` | local-only dev docs | 37 | AG Grid column/grid work | `AGENTS.md` (§11 quirks), `docs/architecture.md` (AG Grid patterns), the feature's `helpers/ag-grid/*.config.ts` | unrelated module docs | 38 | Investigate a bug/incident | `AGENTS.md` (§14 incident log), the area's doc, `HANDOFF.md` | unrelated folder READMEs | 39 | Continue unfinished work | `AGENTS.md`, **`HANDOFF.md`** (present — required), docs it names | docs outside the handoff scope | 40 | Claude Code session | `CLAUDE.md`, then `AGENTS.md` | other docs unless needed | 41 | Docs-only cleanup | `AGENTS.md`, `README.md`, affected `docs/` | source except to verify | 42 43 `HANDOFF.md` currently exists (covers prior notifications/RAG follow-ups) and is required 44 reading for continuation work. Update this table whenever a doc is added, removed, or repurposed. 45 46 --- 47 48 ## 3. Repository / package structure 49 50 | Path | What it is | 51 |---|---| 52 | `src/app/pages/` | **Our code** — all feature modules (RFQ, item library, tracking, admin, etc.) | 53 | `src/app/helpers/` | **Our code** — services, models, HTTP interceptors, AG Grid column configs | 54 | `src/app/components/` | **Our code** — shared reusable components | 55 | `src/app/custom-layout/` | **Our code** — shell layout wrapping all authenticated routes | 56 | `src/app/app-routing.module.ts` | **Our code** — all route definitions and guards | 57 | `src/app/app.module.ts` | **Our code** — root NgModule, providers, interceptors | 58 | `src/environments/` | **Our code** — per-environment API base URL config | 59 | `src/@vex/` | **Framework code** — Vex UI/layout framework (committed into the repo, not a package). Modifiable only with specific justification; avoid casual edits. | 60 | `src/app/pages/rfq/helpers/equation.ts` | **Critical** — RFQ margin/sell-price calculation engine | 61 | `src/app/pages/rfq/helpers/fob-country-autofill.ts` | Pure rule for sub-grid FOB Country auto-fill (never clobbers an existing value) | 62 | `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/` | Vendor-only dialog: download pre-filled xlsx template → fill in → upload back.
